name: Divinity: Original Sin 2 - Definitive Edition
description: The critically acclaimed RPG that raised the bar, from the creators of Baldur's Gate 3. Gather your party. Master deep, tactical combat. Venture as a party of up to four - but know that only one of you will have the chance to become a God.
requirements:
Minimum:
  • Requires a 64-bit processor and operating system
  • OS *: Windows 7 SP1 64-bit or Windows 8.1 64-bit or Windows 10 64-bit
  • Processor: Intel Core i5 or equivalent
  • Memory: 4 GB RAM
  • Graphics: NVIDIA® GeForce® GTX 550 or ATI™ Radeon™ HD 6XXX or higher
  • DirectX: Version 11
  • Storage: 60 GB available space
  • Additional Notes: Minimum requirements may change during development.
comment1:
It would have been a decent RPG had the encounter design not started increasingly relying on immersion-breaking metaknowledge cheese strats as the game went on. Turn one TPKs and gimmick fights are plentiful on Tactician (Act 4 is riddled with them) or you just sometimes lose automatically if you enter the fight directly from dialogue, so you reload the fight knowing about it in advance and only then can you build your strategy, which usually includes stupid pre-buffing and setting up the field while your enemies just stand there doing nothing or you teleport someone far away. Winning strategies made me feel like I was exploiting the game, but the enemies also cheat constantly and can start with 3 times your AP, especially the bosses, which means they can often just kill a party member or two on the first turn if they beat your Initiative. Classic on the other hand is fairly straightforward, but rather lacking in challenge in all those fights that are not just stupid gimmicks, as the AI isn't smart enough nor does it have the stats to survive well-made builds. I would have loved a middle ground between those two difficulties.

The game is also extremely linear because of the artificial gating of areas by level, which means you are forced to do most quests for EXP if you want to progress through the game without abusing one-shot cheese mechanics. The vast stat differences mean being even one level under the enemies' can put you in normally unwinnable situations. 

The plot is a massive slog for how simple it is, the writing is inconsistent and constantly switches between corny humor and grimdark eternal suffering. Everything is so video-gamey, and it is genuinely difficult to RP a consistent character and get immersed in the setting because of the underwhelming dialogue options and the unavoidable save/load hindsight many fights rely on, having to use knowledge my character never could have had to get by.
The inventory management and bartering is absolutely abysmal and lacking in the most basic QoL features, which only wastes your time and ruins the pacing even more. 

The exponentially scaling loot system essentially requiring you to gear up your whole party of FOUR every time you level up is more boring non-gameplay that should never have been in the game. Having to buy or steal spells (your main means of winning any fight) is also incredibly annoying at the start.
All of those things artifically prolong the game, making you spend several hours of your playthrough doing tedious vendor busywork instead of engaging with whatever fun the game has to offer.

As for the pros, the combat system is fun on its own when I don't have to reload my save because the enemy suddenly explodes on death or resurrects itself. The amount of combos and interactions between spells, surfaces, and items allows for a lot of freedom and tactics. Even if it boils down to nuking armor into CC, there are so many ways you can achieve your goals. Alongside the dumb Gotcha! fights, there are also a lot of great battles in this game which you can win by strategy, positioning and properly building your characters. The companions are quite interesting and the first two acts are significantly better than the latter two. The lizards are also my favorite non-human race of all RPGS by far.

Ultimately, I cannot recommend this game, mainly because of how unbalanced, inconsistent and riddled with clutter it is. I must admit that I enjoyed getting through the first half, but its glaring and impossible to ignore flaws become more apparent the further you go.
comment2: very tough start, act one took me 3 trys to get a good run going but past that 10/10 until the start of the final act, and the last fight were bs
comment3: Divinity: Original Sin 2 honestly surprised me with how much depth it has. I expected a good RPG, but I didn’t expect to lose hours just exploring, talking to random characters, and experimenting with different ways to handle quests. The game really rewards creativity, and it feels like there’s never just one “correct” solution to anything. The turn-based combat ended up being way more fun than I thought too. At first it seemed complicated, but once I got used to it, I was completely hooked. Using the environment during fights made every battle feel strategic instead of repetitive. Even small encounters could turn into complete chaos in the best way possible. The writing and companions were probably my favorite part though. The characters actually felt interesting and memorable, and I liked that your decisions affect relationships and the story in noticeable ways. It made me care more about what was happening instead of just rushing through quests. What really makes this game stand out to me is the amount of freedom it gives the player. You can approach situations however you want, and the game usually adapts to it instead of forcing you down one path. It’s rare to find an RPG that feels this open while still having a genuinely good story and fun gameplay. I’d for sure recommend it to anyone who likes story-heavy RPGs or games where your choices actually matter.
